topic: My therapist mailed me fourteen years of session transcripts and I have only ever attended six sessions
title: My Therapist Sent Me Fourteen Years of Transcripts. I Only Attended Six Sessions. | True Scary Story
description_intro: My therapist of fourteen years mailed me a banker's box of session transcripts last September. I have only ever attended six sessions. The other six hundred and twenty four were attended by someone else. Someone with my name, my insurance card, my voice on the recordings, my handwriting on the intake forms.
description:
My therapist of fourteen years mailed me a banker's box of session transcripts last September. I have only ever attended six sessions. The other six hundred and twenty four were attended by someone else. Someone with my name, my insurance card, my voice on the recordings, my handwriting on the intake forms.

Doctor Marie Lindemann had her office in a small medical complex in Whitcomb, Michigan. I started seeing her after my mother passed in two thousand eleven. I stopped seeing her after the sixth visit because I felt I had said what I needed to say. The bills kept arriving for fourteen years. I assumed the insurance was being handled wrong. The bills were never wrong. The insurance was paying for sessions that were actually being held.

There is a recording of one of the sessions I would have called my hundredth, if I had attended any of them. The patient on the recording describes a memory I have never told anyone. Watch to the end. The next session is on Tuesday at four.

#scarystories #truescarystories #horrornarration #analoghorror #scarystoriestotellinthedark #truehorrorstories #creepypasta #identityhorror
tags: scary stories to tell in the dark

sections.narration: My therapist of fourteen years mailed me a banker's box of session transcripts last September. I have only ever attended six of those sessions. The other six hundred and twenty four were attended by someone else. Someone with my name. My insurance card. My voice on the recordings. My handwriting on the intake forms. I have been sitting with the box for four months. I have read forty one of the transcripts. They describe a life I have not lived. They describe a person who is not me. The person uses my name. The therapist calls her by it. She answers to it.

sections.narration: Doctor Marie Lindemann had her office in a small medical complex in Whitcomb, Michigan. Second floor, above a chain pharmacy. Beige carpet, leather chairs, three framed prints of Lake Superior in winter. The waiting room had a fish tank and a wicker basket of magazines on the floor. The reception desk was staffed by a woman named Patricia who had been there for nineteen years. She knew my mother. She greeted me by name the first time I walked in. I have not been back to that office since two thousand twelve. The bills kept coming for fourteen years. I assumed it was a billing error.

sections.narration: I started seeing Doctor Lindemann in March of two thousand eleven. My mother had passed at the end of January. I went to six sessions over four months. We talked about grief. We talked about my father. We talked about my fear of becoming her. After the sixth visit, I told the doctor I felt finished. She agreed. She wished me well. I shook her hand. I drove home and did not go back. The bills kept arriving. I called the insurance company twice. They told me everything was being paid correctly. The therapy was within network. I let it go.

sections.narration: The banker's box arrived last September with no return label. I assumed it was a wedding gift from my sister, mailed late. I opened it on the kitchen counter. Inside were six hundred and thirty file folders. Each was labeled with my name. Each contained typed transcript pages, handwritten intake forms, signed consent documents, and printed receipts for sessions. The earliest dated March of two thousand eleven. The most recent dated August of last year. I sat at the counter and read the most recent one. The transcript described a woman who was struggling to leave her husband. I have never been married.

sections.narration: My first thought was simple. Doctor Lindemann had mixed up two patients with similar names. I called the office and asked for Patricia. She remembered me. She told me Doctor Lindemann had retired in June and wanted her patients to have their files. She read me back the names she had on file under my name. The address was one I had lived at in two thousand sixteen for nine months. The phone number was one I had used for one year. The credit card on file expired last December. I had cancelled that card last December. They had my history. They had me. Not the wrong person.

sections.narration: I sat with the box for two weeks. I read transcripts in order. The first six matched my memory of my sessions. The seventh was held the week after I stopped going. I had no memory of it. The transcript was a woman talking about her mother's death. The grief was specific to the way my mother had died. The transcript continued for thirty pages. The woman cried. She described my father's hands. She described the way my mother held her coffee cup. She described the smell of my mother's hospital room. None of these details have I shared with anyone.

sections.narration: I drove to Whitcomb in October. Patricia let me into Doctor Lindemann's old office. She gave me the key to the file cabinet. The signed intake forms were still inside. Six hundred and thirty of them, one per session. The signatures were identical. They were my signature. I have a peculiar way of crossing my Ts. The crosses are too high, attached to the tops of the letters. Every signature had that high cross. The intake forms were in my handwriting. I write in a slight back slant. They were all in the slight back slant. I checked one against a recent check I had written. Same hand.

sections.narration: The most recent transcripts referenced audio recordings. I called Patricia and asked. She said yes. Every session was recorded with patient consent, stored on a secure server. She sent me login credentials. I logged in that night. I downloaded the recording from session three hundred and forty seven. The voice that answered the doctor's prompt was my voice. Not similar. Not a good impression. Mine. The exact register, the slight Michigan accent, the small breath I take before I begin a sentence. The patient described a memory of being seven and watching a man in the woods behind our house. I have that memory. I have never told anyone.

sections.narration: Doctor Lindemann is dead. I drove to her retirement community in November to ask her directly. She had passed in October from a stroke at the assisted living facility, two weeks after she retired. Her junior associate gave me the news. He had been with the practice for six years. I asked if he had ever met me before. He said yes, that I had been one of his weekly patients since two thousand nineteen. He described what I was wearing. He told me I had a small birthmark on the back of my left hand. I do not have a birthmark on the back of my left hand. I looked. The skin was smooth.

sections.narration: The practice still has me scheduled. Every Tuesday at four. The junior associate calls each month to confirm. I have not gone in. I am told my appointments are still being attended. The recordings continue. The associate sends new transcripts every two weeks. I do not read them anymore. I leave the envelopes sealed in the banker's box. The transcripts describe a person who knows things only I know. She uses my name. She uses my insurance. She has a birthmark on her left hand. I went to my next Tuesday appointment last week. I have not come home. The transcripts from that session arrived this morning. They describe a woman opening a banker's box at her kitchen counter.
